target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts

# GREEN after the ownership fix:

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

target.title: fix: use linked task activity for TaskFlow audit freshness

target.title: refactor(plugins): add lane-oriented channel interface
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: plugin-facing channel runtime and interactive APIs were still shaped around channel-specific surfaces, which pushed plugins toward Telegram/Discord/Slack-specific logic.
- Why it matters: that made portable rich experiences hard to build and blocked a clean fallback model for Slack, Microsoft Teams, Feishu/Lark, Telegram, Discord, and future channels.
- What changed: I added a lane-oriented runtime contract (`PluginLaneRef`, `PluginActorRef`, generic outbound helpers, and `registerInteractionHandler`), extended semantic interactive payloads with action and fallback metadata, and taught bundled channels to project those payloads into native UX when available.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not remove every raw channel runtime namespace yet; they remain host-owned escape hatches, and I kept the implementation plan in a separate commit so it can be dropped cleanly if desired.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature

- [ ] Chore/infra

- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm

### Steps

2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

## Human Verification (required)

- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.


Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed Codex CLI produced JSONL on this machine and both OpenClaw parser paths extracted the final agent message from that transcript. For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

target.title: fix(gateway): cap authenticated websocket identities
target.body:
## Summary
- Cap authenticated gateway websocket sessions per device identity instead of moving every successful handshake into an unbounded shared client set.
- Keep device-less authenticated sessions inside the existing pre-auth budget so shared-auth clients cannot bypass connection limits by omitting device identity.

## Changes
- Added an authenticated connection budget helper with an env-configurable limit and device-scoped acquire/release tracking.
- Enforced the budget during websocket `setClient`, rejecting excess authenticated connections with a policy close before they enter the active client set.
- Added regression coverage for device-scoped limits, budget-slot reclaim after disconnect, device-less authenticated sessions staying under the pre-auth cap, and invalid env fallback handling.

## Validation
- Ran `corepack pnpm test -- src/gateway/server/authenticated-connection-budget.test.ts src/gateway/server.preauth-hardening.test.ts`
- Ran `PATH="/tmp/corepack-bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm build`
- Ran `pnpm check` via the scoped commit hook during both commits
- Ran local agentic review with `claude -p` against the patch/commit and addressed the actionable feedback

## Notes
- Default authenticated connection budget is `8` per device identity, with `OPENCLAW_MAX_AUTHENTICATED_CONNECTIONS_PER_IDENTITY` and `OPENCLAW_TEST_MAX_AUTHENTICATED_CONNECTIONS_PER_IDENTITY` overrides.
- Residual risk: device-less authenticated sessions still rely on the existing pre-auth budget semantics, so their ceiling remains tied to the pre-auth per-IP budget rather than the new device budget.

target.title: fix(discord): record history entry when dropping bot message in allowBots=mentions mode
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `allowBots=mentions` is configured on a Discord account, bot messages that are dropped (no mention) skip `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()`, causing gaps in guild channel history. Other bots in the same channel lose context about dropped messages.
- **Why it matters:** In multi-bot setups, history continuity is critical for context-aware responses. Missing history entries cause bots to "forget" recent bot messages in the channel.
- **What changed:** Added `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` call in the `allowBots=mentions` drop path before `return null`, matching the pattern already used in the `requireMention` drop path.
- **What did NOT change:** No logic change to when messages are dropped; only ensures history is recorded before dropping.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: The `allowBots=mentions` drop path was added without the `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` call that other drop paths already have.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for history recording in the `allowBots=mentions` drop path.
- Prior context: The `requireMention` drop path at line ~900 already records history before returning null.
- Why this regressed now: The `allowBots` feature was added after the history recording pattern was established.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: When a bot message is dropped due to missing mention in `allowBots=mentions` mode, the guild history map should still contain the dropped message entry.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit test directly verifies the history map state after the drop.
- Existing test that already covers this: None previously; added in this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

In multi-bot Discord setups with `allowBots=mentions`, bot messages that are not directly mentioned are now properly recorded in channel history, improving context continuity for other bots.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[bot message, no @mention] -> drop (history NOT recorded) -> other bots lose context

After:
[bot message, no @mention] -> record history -> drop -> other bots retain context
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime: Node.js 22
- Integration/channel: Discord (multi-bot gateway, 6 bots)

### Steps

1. Configure 2+ Discord bot accounts with `allowBots: "mentions"`
2. Bot A sends a message in a shared channel without @mentioning Bot B
3. Bot B drops the message (correct behavior)
4. Check Bot B's guild history for the channel

### Expected

Bot B's guild history contains Bot A's dropped message.

### Actual

Before fix: history entry missing. After fix: history entry recorded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- Test: `pnpm test -- extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts` — "records history when dropping bot message without mention (allowBots=mentions)" passes.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Bot message drop with history recording, bot message with mention (still passes through)
- Edge cases checked: PluralKit bots (exempt from allowBots gate), DM messages (no history recording needed)
- What you did **not** verify: History playback in actual LLM responses across bots

target.title: Add prototype pollution guard to resolveConfigPath
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveConfigPath` in `src/shared/config-eval.ts` traverses config objects by dot-separated path strings (e.g. `"foo.bar.baz"`), but does not check whether any path segment is a dangerous prototype key (`__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor`). This means a crafted path string could be used to access or leak prototype chain properties.

Other path-traversal functions in the codebase already guard against this:
- `parseConfigPath` in `src/config/config-paths.ts`
- `merge-patch.ts`, `runtime-overrides.ts`, `includes.ts`, and others

All of these use the shared `isBlockedObjectKey` utility from `src/infra/prototype-keys.ts`.

## Changes

- Import `isBlockedObjectKey` from `../infra/prototype-keys.js`
- Add a guard inside the `resolveConfigPath` loop that returns `undefined` when a blocked key is encountered
- Matches the existing security pattern used across the codebase

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing tests for `resolveConfigPath` and `isConfigPathTruthyWithDefaults` continue to pass
- [ ] Paths containing `__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor` segments now return `undefined`
- [ ] Normal dot-separated paths still resolve correctly

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `resolveConfigPath` now blocks prototype-key path segments before object property access while preserving normal dot-path resolution.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-59694`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
rg -n "isBlockedObjectKey|blocks prototype keys" src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
src/shared/config-eval.test.ts:62:  it("blocks prototype keys while resolving config paths", () => {
src/shared/config-eval.ts:4:import { isBlockedObjectKey } from "../infra/prototype-keys.js";
src/shared/config-eval.ts:31:    if (isBlockedObjectKey(part)) {
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched resolver has a real guard before object access and the regression coverage keeps `safe.enabled` resolving while `__proto__`, `constructor.name`, and `prototype.polluted` return `undefined`.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `b11430d` (after the OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via `shortenHomePath`, which only redacts the *effective* OpenClaw home. When `OPENCLAW_HOME` points to a directory other than the OS home, a Node binary under the OS home was left unredacted, leaking the OS username into copied Doctor output. The latest commit adds a second-pass OS-home redaction (POSIX + Windows) and keeps the contribution detect-only.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `b11430d`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head, OS-home redaction shown):** The first block is the real render on this machine. The second block is the redaction scenario this commit fixes: with `OPENCLAW_HOME` set elsewhere and Node living under the user's OS home, the exec path is collapsed to `~/...` so the OS username is not leaked. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios computed against the real current date.
```
=== Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
OPENCLAW_HOME      : /srv/openclaw
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 25 (25.6.1, in maintenance) ===
Node 25 is in maintenance mode (EOL 2026-06-01, ~1 month remaining).
Consider upgrading to a current Active LTS release for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~11 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). In the redaction scenario, with `OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw` and Node under the user's OS home, the rendered exec path is `~/.nvm/...` — the OS username does not appear. Before this commit, `shortenHomePath` alone returned the path unchanged (it only redacts the OpenClaw home `/srv/openclaw`, not the OS home), so the username leaked. Injected Node 25 / 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX and Windows `OPENCLAW_HOME`-override regression tests cover the redaction case; 50 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: feat(doctor): detect volatile filesystem (tmpfs/ramfs/overlay) for state directory
target.body:
## Summary

Add detection for state directories on volatile filesystems (tmpfs, ramfs, overlay) that lose all data on reboot. This complements the existing SD/eMMC storage and iCloud cloud-sync detection in `doctor-state-integrity`.

## Problem

Container deployments (Docker, LXC) and certain Linux configurations may place the state directory on tmpfs, ramfs, or an overlay root filesystem. When this happens, sessions, credentials, and config are silently destroyed on every reboot. Doctor currently checks for cloud-synced dirs (macOS) and SD/eMMC media (Linux) but has no detection for volatile filesystems.

## Solution

Reuse the existing `/proc/self/mountinfo` parsing infrastructure (`parseLinuxMountInfo` + `findLinuxMountInfoEntryForPath`) to check whether the state directory's filesystem type is in the volatile set (`tmpfs`, `ramfs`, `overlay`).

### Modified: `src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.ts`
- `LinuxVolatileStateDir` type — mirrors `LinuxSdBackedStateDir` structure
- `VOLATILE_FS_TYPES` — `Set` of volatile filesystem type strings
- `detectLinuxVolatileStateDir()` — reuses mountinfo parsing, checks fsType against volatile set
- `formatLinuxVolatileStateDirWarning()` — follows existing warning format conventions
- `noteStateIntegrity()` — register volatile-fs warning between SD/eMMC and state-dir-exists checks

### New: `src/commands/doctor-volatile-fs.test.ts` (10 tests)
- tmpfs / ramfs / overlay detection
- ext4 non-detection (no false positive)
- Non-linux platform skip
- Null mountInfo handling
- Most-specific mount point selection
- No false positive when `/tmp` is tmpfs but state dir is on ext4
- Warning format validation

## Design decisions

1. **Reuse existing infrastructure** — `parseLinuxMountInfo` and `findLinuxMountInfoEntryForPath` already solve mount-point matching. No new parsing code needed.
2. **Same dependency injection pattern** — `deps?` parameter with `platform`, `mountInfo`, `resolveRealPath` for testability, consistent with `detectLinuxSdBackedStateDir`.
3. **overlay included** — Docker's default storage driver uses overlay2/overlay. A state directory on an overlay root without a volume mount will lose data.
4. **Warning placement** — after SD/eMMC check, before state-dir-exists check, so volatile-fs warning appears alongside other storage-quality warnings.

## Verification

- `pnpm check`: 0 errors
- `pnpm vitest run src/commands/doctor-volatile-fs.test.ts`: 10/10 passed

---


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw doctor had no detection for state directories on volatile filesystems (tmpfs/ramfs). On such mounts, sessions, credentials, and config are silently destroyed on reboot. This patch adds a state-integrity warning for that case.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), OpenClaw built from this PR head (`openclaw doctor` run from source). `/run/user/1000` is a real `tmpfs` mount per `/proc/self/mountinfo`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
$ grep '/run/user/1000 ' /proc/self/mountinfo
169 132 0:78 / /run/user/1000 rw,nosuid,nodev,relatime shared:235 - tmpfs tmpfs rw,size=1637856k,...

$ mkdir -p /run/user/1000/openclaw-vol-test
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/run/user/1000/openclaw-vol-test openclaw doctor
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Live `openclaw doctor` terminal output, "State integrity" section, when the state dir is on the tmpfs mount:
```
State integrity
  - State directory is on a volatile filesystem
    (/run/user/1000/openclaw-vol-test; fs tmpfs, mount /run/user/1000).
  - Sessions, credentials, and config will be lost on reboot.
  - Move OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a persistent filesystem to avoid data loss.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Running `openclaw doctor` with the state dir on the tmpfs mount emits the volatile-filesystem warning shown above. Running `openclaw doctor` with the default `~/.openclaw` (ext4) emits no such warning, confirming no false positive. The warning fires only for `tmpfs`/`ramfs` mounts.

- **What was not tested:** ramfs was not exercised on a live mount (tmpfs and ramfs share the same code path via the volatile fs-type set); macOS and Windows paths are skipped by design as the check is Linux-only.

target.title: fix: Config Raw mode permanently disabled due to round-trip check regression
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #59330 — Control UI Config editor permanently shows "Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text)" since 2026.3.31.

## Root Cause

Two issues cause `shouldFallbackToStructuredRawRedaction()` to always return `true`:

### 1. `normalizeExecSafeBinProfilesInConfig` assigns `void 0` instead of deleting keys

When `safeBinProfiles`/`safeBinTrustedDirs` are empty, the normalize function assigns `undefined`:

```ts
typedExec.safeBinProfiles = ... ? normalizedProfiles : undefined;
```

This creates keys with `undefined` values. JSON-parsed objects can never have `undefined`-valued keys, so `isDeepStrictEqual()` always fails:

```ts
isDeepStrictEqual({ safeBinProfiles: undefined }, {}) // → false
```

### 2. `shouldFallbackToStructuredRawRedaction` compares against materialized config

The round-trip check compares the restored (raw-parsed) config against `snapshot.config` (the **materialized** runtime config), which contains transformations not present in the raw source:

- `~/` paths expanded to absolute paths
- Model `api` field inherited from provider level
- Auto-generated `alias` fields on model entries
- Default empty `config: {}` added to plugin entries

The comparison should use `snapshot.parsed` (the source config before materialization).

## Changes

1. **`src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts`**: Use `delete` instead of assigning `undefined` when profiles/dirs are empty.

2. **`src/config/redact-snapshot.raw.ts`**:
   - Accept optional `sourceConfig` parameter for comparison target
   - Normalize both sides with a recursive filter to strip any remaining `undefined`-valued keys

3. **`src/config/redact-snapshot.ts`**: Pass `snapshot.parsed` as `sourceConfig` to the round-trip check.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Control UI Config editor permanently disabled the Raw tab and showed "Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text)." since openclaw 2026.3.31. Tracked in #59330.

**Real environment tested**: Globally-installed openclaw 2026.5.18 on macOS (Darwin 25.5.0, arm64). Patch applied directly to the installed `dist/` bundles (`normalize-exec-safe-bin` + `redact-snapshot` modules) to mirror the source-level fix in this PR. Gateway restarted, Control UI opened via `openclaw dashboard`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Apply the patch to the installed `dist/` bundles (mirrors this PR's source changes), then restart the running gateway so it loads the patched code:

```bash
./hotfix-toggle.sh on
pkill -f 'openclaw/dist/index.js gateway'
openclaw gateway start
```

2. Open the Control UI and switch the Config editor to Raw mode:

```bash
openclaw dashboard
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Before — Control UI Config editor with Raw tab permanently disabled (reproduces the regression in #59330):

![Control UI Config editor with Raw tab disabled and "Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text)." message](https://github.com/rayseling/openclaw/releases/download/proof-pr-59336/raw-mode-before.png)

After — Raw tab enabled, raw JSON5 config renders with 5 sensitive values redacted (`__OPENCLAW_REDACTED__`) as expected:

![Control UI Config editor with Raw tab enabled, showing "Raw config (JSON/JSON5)" panel with "5 secrets redacted" badge and "5 sensitive values hidden. Use the reveal button above to edit the raw config." advisory](https://github.com/rayseling/openclaw/releases/download/proof-pr-59336/raw-mode-after.png)

**Observed result after fix**: The Raw tab in the Control UI Config editor is enabled. The panel renders the redacted raw JSON5 with the "5 secrets redacted" badge and the "5 sensitive values hidden. Use the reveal button above to edit the raw config." advisory. Toggling between Form and Raw works without the fallback message. `snapshot.raw` is a string instead of `null` after the patch.

**What was not tested**: Behavior with `agents[].tools.exec.safeBinProfiles` populated (only the empty-profiles regression path that triggered the bug was exercised). The short-sensitive-value collision reported by @zhangqinghua2015 in a comment on this PR is a separate raw-redaction edge case and is intentionally out of scope here.

---

🤖 AI-assisted (Claude via OpenClaw). Fully tested locally against real config. I understand what the code does.

target.title: feat(plugins): add guard delivery and subagent review hooks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the current hook surface can gate tool execution, but it cannot synchronously guard tool results before the agent sees them and it has no explicit pre-spawn / pre-return subagent review phases.
- Why it matters: phase 1-3 guard work needs a typed decision model plus real observation and subagent boundaries, otherwise "auto mode" and third-party policy plugins sit on incomplete seams.
- What changed: added shared guard decision/annotation types, new `before_tool_result_deliver` / `before_subagent_spawn` / `before_subagent_result_deliver` hooks, wired them into tool-result persistence and subagent spawn/announce flows, and extended targeted tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add a guard kernel, model-backed policy runtime, or any bundled automode/firewall plugin yet.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): N/A
- Why this regressed now: N/A
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/wired-hooks-subagent.test.ts`, `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.tool-result-persist-hook.test.ts`, `src/agents/sessions-spawn-hooks.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: guard decisions merge consistently, tool results can be warned/blocked before persistence, subagent spawn can be denied before launch, and child results can be rewritten before announce delivery.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests hit the exact hook seams without needing the full policy stack.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing hook wiring tests cover the older lifecycle hooks only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins can now return typed guard decisions and annotations from `before_tool_call`.
- Plugins can synchronously review and rewrite/block tool results before the next model turn via `before_tool_result_deliver`.
- Plugins can synchronously review/block subagent spawn and child-result delivery via `before_subagent_spawn` and `before_subagent_result_deliver`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees result]
[subagent spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [subagent announce]

After:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [before_tool_result_deliver] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees guarded result]
[subagent spawn] -> [before_subagent_spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [before_subagent_result_deliver] -> [subagent announce]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: new hook phases can block or rewrite execution/results, but they only extend existing plugin authority. Mitigation is typed decision shapes, synchronous ordering, and targeted seam coverage around the new hook paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local repo config

### Steps

1. Register a plugin hook on `before_tool_result_deliver`, `before_subagent_spawn`, or `before_subagent_result_deliver`.
2. Run the corresponding tool/subagent flow.
3. Observe the result warning/block/rewrite before transcript persistence or subagent delivery.

### Expected

- New hook phases run synchronously and can warn/deny/rewrite at the intended seam.

### Actual

- Implemented locally; full repo harness remained slow in this shell, so verification is limited to targeted code review, file-by-file smoke imports for lighter modules, and staged targeted tests added in this PR.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed all new hook registration types and runner wiring, confirmed new hook names are in the public hook union/list, confirmed spawn/result guard call sites are wired, confirmed branch/commit contains the targeted tests.
- Edge cases checked: deny vs escalate handling, sync-hook Promise guard, warning annotation merge path, blocked child-result fallback text.
- What you did **not** verify: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm tsgo` completion in this shell; bounded `vitest` and `tsc` invocations timed out without surfacing a specific failure.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: plugins may start relying on the new guard decision semantics before a stronger core reducer/guard kernel exists.
  - Mitigation: the new phases are explicit and typed, and this PR keeps the policy surface narrow instead of introducing plugin-owned model runtimes.
- Risk: result rewriting/blocking changes what reaches the transcript and agent context.
  - Mitigation: the change is isolated to explicit hook opt-in and covered by targeted persistence/announce tests.

target.title: UI: fix half-block art rendering for UTF-8 QR codes in web chat
target.body:
## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: UTF-8 QR codes (using half-block characters ▀▄█) render broken in the web chat dashboard — default `
` line-height creates gaps between rows, background/foreground colors don't match the block-character encoding, and the characters don't tile into a scannable image.
- Why it matters: QR codes sent by tools (e.g. Algorand ARC-26 QR generator) are unreadable/unscannable in the web UI, forcing users to copy raw text and render elsewhere.
- What changed: Added `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` detection in `markdown.ts` that identifies code blocks dominated by half-block Unicode characters, wraps them with a `.half-block-art` CSS class, and applies tight line-height (0.6), black background, white foreground, and slightly larger font-size so the blocks tile seamlessly. CSS added for both `.chat-text` (chat messages) and `.sidebar-markdown` (tool output sidebar), with both light and dark theme variants.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the markdown parsing pipeline, sanitization, or any other code block rendering. Only half-block-art detection and styling were added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Code blocks containing UTF-8 half-block art (QR codes, box-drawing art) now render with black background, white text, and tight line-height so they display as intended scannable images.
- No change to normal code block rendering.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS, Ubuntu
- Runtime/container: TUI, Browser (web chat dashboard)
- Model/provider: Any (content is tool output)
- Integration/channel (if any): Web UI
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Open the web chat dashboard
2. Have a tool or assistant send a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. via Algorand `generate_algorand_qrcode` or any tool that outputs Unicode block-character QR codes)
3. Observe the code block rendering

### Expected

- QR code renders as a clean, scannable square image with no gaps between rows

### Actual

- Before: rows have visible gaps due to default line-height, background is gray instead of black, characters don't tile — QR is unreadable
- After: QR code renders correctly and is scannable

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: UTF-8 QR codes built from half-block characters (▀▄█) rendered broken in the web chat dashboard — the default `
` line-height left gaps between rows and the gray background/foreground did not match the block-character encoding, so the codes were unscannable. This patch detects half-block art, wraps it in a `.half-block-art` class, and applies tight line-height, black background, white foreground, and larger font-size so the blocks tile into a clean scannable image, across both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces in light and dark themes.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw web chat dashboard running locally on macOS and Ubuntu (Node 22), rendering live tool output that emits a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. the Algorand ARC-26 `generate_algorand_qrcode` tool).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Opened the web chat dashboard, had a tool emit a UTF-8 half-block QR code, then viewed it in both the chat message view and the tool-output sidebar, in light and dark themes, and scanned the rendered code with a phone camera.

Evidence after fix:

**Before**



**After**





Observed result after fix: The QR code renders as a clean, gap-free square and scans correctly with a phone camera in both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces; normal code blocks remain unaffected.

What was not tested: Terminal-only (TUI) rendering paths and exhaustive QR color combinations; the detection heuristic may not cover every possible block-art edge case.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: QR code rendering in chat messages and tool output sidebar, both light and dark themes
- Edge cases checked: Normal code blocks unaffected, short block-character snippets (< 10 lines) do not trigger detection, non-QR code blocks render as before
- What you did **not** verify: All possible QRCode coloring; detection heuristic may not cover all edge cases of block art

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `.half-block-art` CSS rules in `text.css` and `sidebar.css`, and remove the `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` branch in `markdown.ts`
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/styles/chat/text.css`, `ui/src/styles/chat/sidebar.css`, `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Normal code blocks accidentally styled as half-block art (false positive detection), or QR codes still rendering with gaps (false negative)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: False positive detection on code blocks that happen to contain many block characters but aren't QR/art
  - Mitigation: Detection requires 60%+ of lines to contain half-block characters AND at least 10 lines — unlikely for normal code. Worst case is cosmetic (black bg + tight line-height on a non-QR block).

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: Plugins: enforce private import boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins could couple directly to private OpenClaw internals or escape their package root through imports, even though the intended contract is the plugin SDK seam.
- Why it matters: That makes plugin behavior depend on unstable host internals and weakens the architecture boundary between plugin code and core.
- What changed: Added an enforced loader-time import policy that allows `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`, declared package dependencies, Node builtins, and in-package relative imports, while blocking private `openclaw/*` imports, undeclared bare packages, and imports that escape the plugin root.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not add process isolation or a sandbox; plugins still run arbitrary JS in-process.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Third-party and bundled plugins now fail to load when they import private OpenClaw package paths, undeclared bare packages, or files outside their own package root.
- Plugin authors can continue using declared dependencies, Node builtins, and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` seams.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Plugin code can no longer import private OpenClaw internals or escape its package root through the loader import path. This is contract hardening, not malicious-plugin sandboxing.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin loader
- Relevant config (redacted): local temp plugin fixtures

### Steps

1. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/runtime` or `../outside.cjs`.
2. Load it through the plugin loader.
3. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`, a declared dependency, and an in-package helper.

### Expected

- Private OpenClaw imports and root-escaping imports are rejected before plugin execution.
- Declared dependencies, SDK seams, and in-package helpers continue to load.

### Actual

- Matches expected in direct local probes.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm build`
  - repo `pnpm check` pipeline via `scripts/committer`
  - direct `node --import tsx` probe rejecting `openclaw/runtime`
  - direct `node --import tsx` probe allowing declared dependency + local helper import
- Edge cases checked:
  - plugin root escape via relative import
  - declared dependency allowlist from plugin-local `package.json`
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` suite completion in this worktree
  - real third-party plugins yet

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Mostly yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Maybe
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Plugin authors importing private OpenClaw internals must move to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` or declare/package their own dependencies locally.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - Revert commit `6a8e3526fa`
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/plugins/import-policy.ts`
  - `src/plugins/loader.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - plugins failing at load with `plugin import policy violation`
  - plugins relying on undeclared dependencies or private `openclaw/*` imports

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Existing plugins may rely on undeclared bare packages or private host imports and now fail fast.
  - Mitigation:
  - The failure is early and explicit, and the allowed path is narrow: declare the dependency or move to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as “disable switchable session history”.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target session’s stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: fix(security): use constant-time comparison for TLS fingerprint verification
target.body:
## Summary

TLS certificate fingerprint verification in `src/gateway/client.ts` uses `!==` (standard JavaScript string equality), which is vulnerable to **timing side-channel attacks** (CWE-208).

An attacker on the network path can measure response time differences to iteratively guess the correct fingerprint byte-by-byte, potentially bypassing TLS pinning for MITM attacks on gateway connections.

## Root cause

Two locations perform fingerprint comparison with `!==`:

| Location | Context |
|----------|---------|
| Line 205 | WebSocket `checkServerIdentity` callback |
| Line 681 | HTTP `validateTlsFingerprint` method |

## Fix

Replace both with `safeEqualSecret()` from `src/security/secret-equal.ts`, which uses `SHA-256` hashing + `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time comparison.

This is the **same function already used** for pairing token verification elsewhere in the codebase — just missed for TLS fingerprints.

```diff
- if (fingerprint !== expected) {
+ if (!safeEqualSecret(fingerprint, expected)) {
```

## Why safeEqualSecret and not raw timingSafeEqual

`timingSafeEqual` requires both buffers to have equal length, which would leak the length of the expected fingerprint. `safeEqualSecret` avoids this by hashing both values with SHA-256 first (producing fixed 32-byte digests), then comparing the digests in constant time.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — compiles cleanly
- [x] `pnpm check` — lint/format passes
- [x] Existing `safeEqualSecret` tests in `src/security/audit-extra.sync.test.ts` cover equal, unequal, different-length, and undefined inputs
- [x] No remaining `fingerprint !== expected` patterns in codebase
